title: Listening - Part 2
description: You will hear an acoustic engineer called Abbi Grant talking about the design of public spaces. For questions 7 – 14, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.
instructions: You will hear an acoustic engineer called Abbi Grant talking about the design of public spaces. For questions 7 – 14, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.

extracts.context: Abbi Grant discusses how the sonic environment of a building affects human behavior and well-being.
extracts.audio_script:
Hi, I’m Abbi. When we think of architecture, we usually think about what we see. But as an acoustic engineer, I think about what we hear. My interest in this field didn't start in a lecture hall; it actually began when I visited a [7]grand cathedral[/7] as a child. I was fascinated by how a single whisper could travel across a massive stone space. It made me realize that buildings have a voice of their own.

Modern open-plan offices are an acoustic nightmare. I’ve found that the [8]background chatter[/8] is the primary cause of stress among employees. It’s not necessarily the volume, but the lack of privacy. We use 'sound masking' systems that emit a gentle, neutral noise—similar to a fan—to cover up the sound of human speech. It allows people to focus without being constantly interrupted by their colleagues' conversations.

In hospitals, acoustics can actually affect recovery times. I noticed that [9]hard surfaces[/9] like tiles and glass reflect sound, creating a chaotic environment that prevents patients from sleeping. By replacing these with sound-absorbent materials, we can create a much calmer atmosphere. We are learning that silence is just as important as medicine in the healing process.

We also design spaces for performance. For a concert hall, the [10]reverberation time[/10] is the most critical metric we measure. If the sound lasts too long, the music becomes muddy; if it’s too short, it feels 'dead'. We use adjustable panels to fine-tune the room depending on whether they are playing a solo violin or a full orchestra. It’s a precision science.

Urban noise is a growing problem. I’ve found that [11]green barriers[/11]—like walls of plants—are much more effective than concrete fences at reducing traffic noise. They don't just block the sound; they diffuse it. Plus, the visual presence of nature has a psychological effect that makes people perceive the remaining noise as less annoying.

Technology is giving us new tools. I noticed that [12]augmented reality[/12] is now being used to let architects 'hear' a building before it’s even built. They can walk through a digital model and experience the acoustics of different materials in real-time. It’s preventing many expensive mistakes at the construction stage.

Looking forward, I believe that 'personal sound zones' will become a reality in public transport. I’m concerned that the [13]privacy invasion[/13] of people playing music out loud on buses will lead to more conflict. New directional speaker technology can focus sound into a tiny area, meaning you can listen to a movie without headphones and the person next to you won't hear a thing.

Finally, I’m currently writing a [14]city guide[/14] that highlights the quietest spots in London. I want to help people find places where they can escape the noise and just breathe. Thank you for your time.
